title: Mayor Wu remains firm as councilors consider rejecting her budget
author: Ross Cristantiello
bullets: Mayor Wu's $4.9 billion budget proposal faces potential rejection from Boston City Council members seeking increased spending.
Councilors have until June 10 to accept, amend, or reject Wu's budget; a rejection may not lead to increased spending.
Boston faces a $50 million budget deficit, influenced by snow removal, police overtime, and health insurance costs.

title: Scholars bar is closing after 15 years. A new concept is already planned.
author: Autumn Sloboda
bullets: Scholars American Bistro & Cocktail Lounge in Downtown Crossing is closing after 15 years.
The bar will host two sendoff events this weekend titled 'The Last Friday' and 'One Last Night'.
The space will reopen as Off The Rails Boston, a live music-focused restaurant, in late summer.

title: How much are you spending to attend the World Cup?
author: Annie Jonas
bullets: Boston hosts seven FIFA World Cup 2026 matches starting June 13 at the temporarily renamed Boston Stadium.
Round-trip Commuter Rail tickets from South Station to Foxborough cost $80, with shuttle buses up to $95 round-trip.
Hotel and Airbnb prices in Greater Boston, especially Dorchester and Providence, have surged for World Cup dates.

title: A first look at the massive outdoor bar coming to Cape Cod this summer
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: The Backyard by Foley’s, a new outdoor bar, is set to open in Dennis Port on Cape Cod this summer.
The 33,650 square-foot venue will feature a 5,000 square-foot sail-covered main bar and various seating options.
The venue will include rotating food trucks, live music, activity zones, and collaborations with local businesses.

title: Chelsea man pleads not guilty after allegedly threatening BU campus, students, deans
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: Maximillien Sajous, 29, a BU student, pleaded not guilty to threatening the BU campus, students, and deans.
Sajous faces felony charges including stalking and bomb/hijack threat; he's held without bail due to dangerousness concerns.
He was allegedly involved in mass shooting threats and had a summer intern position rescinded by the Attorney General's Office.

title: Healey refuses to alter RMV policy that denies undercover license plates to ICE
author: Ross Cristantiello
bullets: Gov. Maura Healey refuses to change RMV's policy denying undercover license plates to most ICE vehicles.
The Trump administration threatens legal action, claiming the policy endangers ICE agents and violates constitutional law.
Healey's spokesperson argues the RMV policy prevents ICE from operating secretly and violating people's rights in Massachusetts.

title: Body of Quincy kayaker pulled from Boston Harbor
author: Abby Patkin
bullets: Tri Trinh, 72, found dead in Boston Harbor after kayaking, no trauma or foul play suspected.
First responders retrieved Trinh's body near Thompson Island; he started kayaking from a Quincy beach.
Authorities sent Trinh's body to Beth Israel Deaconess Hospital; autopsy results are pending.

title: South Shore school employee pleads guilty to stealing food, equipment for ‘Snack Shack’
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: Plymouth's former director of food services, Patrick Van Cott, pleaded guilty to stealing school resources for his business 'Snack Shack.'
Van Cott used federal and school funds to buy equipment and food, diverting items like refrigerators and premium burgers to his cafe.
He faces sentencing in August, with potential penalties including up to 20 years in prison and a $500,000 fine.

title: New Allston hotel wins design award
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: The Atlas Hotel in Allston won the Lifestyle Hotel Public Space category at the 22nd Annual Hospitality Design Awards.
Located near Harvard's Enterprise Research Campus, the hotel features a lobby inspired by Ray Oldenburg's 'third places' concept.
Additional amenities include a fitness center, meeting spaces, restaurant, and a rooftop bar, Foxglove Terrace.

title: JetBlue is ending service at Manchester-Boston Regional Airport
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: JetBlue will end service at Manchester-Boston Regional Airport on July 8, 2026, due to a 'capacity crisis.'
Manchester airport officials worked to promote JetBlue but cited high jet fuel prices as a challenge.
Customers on affected flights can choose alternate JetBlue flights or receive a full refund from the airline.
